coredata.dc:title: Selective detection of manganese(II) ions based on the fluorescence turn-on response via histidine functionalized carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mohagheghpour, Elham
Farzin, Leila
Ghoorchian, Arash
Sadjadi, Sodeh
Abdouss, Majid
coredata.dc:description: Herein, water-soluble emissive carbon quantum dots (His-CQDs) were synthesized from pyrolysis of sodium citrate in the presence of histidine under hydrothermal conditions. The as-synthesized His-CQDs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR), fluorescence spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering (DLS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques. The obtained His-CQDs display a strong emission peak at 534 nm when excited at 476 nm with a high quantum yield (61.8 %). The as-synthesized His-CQDs were applied as a new platform for highly selective determination of Mn(II) based on the fluorescence “turn-on” response with a limit of detection of 1.85 µg L−1 (at 3σ) and a linear range of 3.50–35.5 µg L−1 in aqueous solution. The sensing mechanism of the His-CQDs probe for the detection of Mn(II) was studied via density functional theory (DFT), FT-IR, and EDTA complexation methodology. In addition, His-CQDs were successfully applied to determine the accurate amounts of Mn(II) in whole blood control material. More importantly, the integrating such an efficient sensor with point-of-care technology can enable portable, easy-to-use, and rapid sensing systems for better biological and clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensor
Histidine functionalized carbon quantum dots
Manganese (II)
Whole blood samples

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic overview of the preparation process of His-CQDs.

(A) TEM image, (B) size distribution profile, (C) FT-IR spectrum, (D) XPS survey spectrum, (E) absorbance spectrum, (F) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, (G) excitation-dependent emission spectra (360–600 nm) of His-CQDs. (H) The variation of fluorescence intensity (Curve 1) and emission wavelength values of the His-CQDs probe (Curve 2) with different excitation wavelength.

The effect of (A) time and (B) NaCl concentration on the photophysical properties of His-CQDs (0.18 g of the His-CQDs in 1 mL Millipore water (A) or NaCl solution (B)). The fluorescence spectra were recorded at λem = 534 nm, where λex = 476 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of His-CQDs probe in the presence of a) blank, Fe(III), Al(III), Zn(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), Cr(III), Cd(II), Ca(II), Mg(II), As(V), Hg(II), Au(III), Bi(III), Cr(VI), Se(IV), Sn(IV), V(V), Ni(II), b) Co(II), and c) Mn(II). (B) Fluorescence response of the probe towards different metal ions. The concentration of tested ions: 23 µg L−1. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at λem = 534 nm, where λex = 476 nm.

The fluorescence intensity of the His-CQDs (0.18 g in 1 mL Millipore water) at various pHs in the presence of Mn(II) (23 µg L−1). The fluorescence spectra were recorded at λem = 534 nm, where λex = 476 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of His-CQDs for various concentrations of Mn(II) (3.50 – 35.5 μmol L−1). (B) The corresponding relationships between the fluorescence intensity at 534 nm and concentrations of Mn(II).

(A) FT-IR spectra of His-QCDs in the presence and absence of Mn(II). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of His-QCDs (0.18 g of the His-CQDs in 1 mL of distilled water) in the presence of Mn(II) ion (20 μg L−1) and Mn(II) ion + EDTA (87.6 μg L−1) in aqueous solution at room temperature. (C) Optimized minimum energy geometries for histidine complexes with the Cr(II), Cr(III), Cu(II), Fe(II), Cd(II), and Mn(II) at the B3LYP/6-31G(d,p)-(LANL2DZ) level of theory.
The comparison of the proposed method and other published works in the determination of Mn(II) ion.

head.ce:title: Selective detection of manganese(II) ions based on the fluorescence turn-on response via histidine functionalized carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Radiation Application Research School, Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Radiation Application Research School, Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Radiation Application Research School, Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Hamadan University of Medical Sciences, Hamadan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Hamadan University of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Hamadan University of Medical Sciences, Hamadan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, water-soluble emissive carbon quantum dots (His-CQDs) were synthesized from pyrolysis of sodium citrate in the presence of histidine under hydrothermal conditions. The as-synthesized His-CQDs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR), fluorescence spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering (DLS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques. The obtained His-CQDs display a strong emission peak at 534 nm when excited at 476 nm with a high quantum yield (61.8 %). The as-synthesized His-CQDs were applied as a new platform for highly selective determination of Mn(II) based on the fluorescence “turn-on” response with a limit of detection of 1.85 µg L−1 (at 3σ) and a linear range of 3.50–35.5 µg L−1 in aqueous solution. The sensing mechanism of the His-CQDs probe for the detection of Mn(II) was studied via density functional theory (DFT), FT-IR, and EDTA complexation methodology. In addition, His-CQDs were successfully applied to determine the accurate amounts of Mn(II) in whole blood control material. More importantly, the integrating such an efficient sensor with point-of-care technology can enable portable, easy-to-use, and rapid sensing systems for better biological and clinical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As the eleventh most abundant element, manganese is widely distributed in the environment and is an essential nutrient that plays various vital biological roles within the human body. For example, manganese contributes to various enzymes functions, bone growth, and is required for the proper function of the nervous and immune systems [1,2]. Moreover, it is a very versatile scientific and diagnostic tool, and it has proven helpful in biological and neurobiological research [3,4]. However, when present in the body at high concentrations, it may result in mood swings, cognitive disorders, and other neuronal defects, which at advanced stages produces a Parkinson’s disease-like syndrome [5]. The safety limit of manganese in the human body set by the World Health Organization (WHO) is 50 µg L−1 [6]. In light of the broad utility of Mn(II) in living systems, it is of interest to develop analytical tools for its detection [7]. Moreover, owing to the medicinal effects of manganese and its biological roles, this substance finds extensive applications in therapeutic supplements and its determination is very important in pharmaceutical industry [8].

Manganese is introduced to the environment by both natural enrichment from rocks and soil, and by human activities such as from dry alkaline battery production, agricultural fungicides, steel production, and ferro/silico-Mn alloy production. Surface and groundwater pollution is one of the terrible consequences of environmental manganese exposure, which can cause adverse negative effects to human health and the ecosystem. Thus, regular monitoring of manganese in water is very important [9]. Accordingly, it is of significance to develop a fast, sensitive, low-cost, and short-time method and appropriate limit of detection (LOD) for Mn(II) detection. Inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) [10], stripping voltammetry [11], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [10], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [12], and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy [13] are among the common analytical methods to determine Mn(II). However, these techniques present disadvantages of requiring high cost, sophisticated instruments, complex procedures, and high-level operation skills. Additionally, they are not feasible to carry out in-loco analyses [14]. Compared to other conventional analytical techniques, optical sensing using fluorescence is a powerful tool for detecting trace metals due to its high sensitivity, and simplicity of detection [15]. The great advantage of fluorescence spectroscopy over other techniques such as absorption spectroscopy is that fluorescence is a background-free technique, so very small concentrations of analyte can be detected.

To date, only a few fluorescent sensors for the detection of Mn(II) have been reported. The main obstacle in the design and development of Mn(II) fluorescent sensors lies in the paramagnetic nature of Mn(II), which leads to fluorescence quenching [16] and the lack of Mn-selective ligands [3]. Most of the sensing strategies for Mn(II) were designed based on a selective sensing material such as 1,2-bis(o-aminophenoxy)ethane-N,N,N,N-tetraacetic acid [17], a benzil and 2-amino pyridine based fluorescence probe [18], a new Schiff-base fluorescence probe [3], and a macrocyclic ligand [16]. Unfortunately, all of the mentioned examples require complex synthesis processes [14].

As a new type of luminophores, quantum dots (QDs) find extensive applications in the detection of trace amounts of different analytes because of their unique optical properties. The common advantages of QDs are the size-tunable fluorescence, their brightness, and photochemical stability [19,20]. For instance, Xu et al. reported a novel discriminative detection of Mn(II) via CdTe QD fluorescence sensing [21]. It should be mentioned that the toxicity of most of QDs has limited their wide applications [22]. One possible solution is to shift from conventional toxic semiconductor QDs (such as Cd- and Pd-based QDs) to carbon-based QDs (CQDs), which are not only non-toxic, green, biocompatible, with a surface that is decorated with different oxygen- containing functional groups (causing a suitable dispersive property of CQDs in aqueous phases) but also are NIR active and demonstrate infrared up-conversion properties, which are desirable for bio-imaging applications [23,24].

Researchers are extensively investigating the different methods to modulate the CQDs photophysical properties through controlling the size of CQDs using different synthesis methodology, passivation, the modification of core material, and surface modification of CQDs [25–32]. The presence of reaction sites on the surface of CQDs provides a unique capacity for attachment of other functionalizing agents that can be used for specific targeting [33].

An interesting strategy for the design of fluorescent sensors relies on the use of a chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) mechanism, in which specific covalent bond-formation of coordinating groups of luminophores with metal ions can trigger an increase in fluorescence emission. Metal binding must alter the electronic and/or molecular structure of the sensor, which is reflected in a change of its fluorescence properties. For example, involvement of the lone electron pair on donor atoms in forming bonds to metal ions lessens the photoinduced electron transfer quenching effect, resulting in a large increase in fluorescence intensity upon metal binding [34,35].

Drawing inspiration from these ideas, we report a one-pot synthesis of water-soluble histidine functionalized CQDs (named as His-CQDs) via a hydrothermal method with histidine and sodium citrate as the precursors. The photostability, pH stability, aqueous dispersibility, and salt effect were investigated. The different metal ions were tested for metal ion interaction selectivity with His-CQDs surface functional groups, but only Mn(II) caused a remarkable fluorescence enhancement. Based on this excellent property, we further evaluated the effects of factors on the fluorescence response, such as pH values, reaction time, initial ion concentration, and the presence of interfering metal ions. Finally, the practical application of His-CQDs for detecting metal ions was examined to detect Mn(II) in real samples such as whole blood.

All reagents used were of analytical grade or with the highest quality and were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Standard stock solutions of metal ions were prepared by the dilution of Titrisol standard metal salt solutions purchased fromMerck (Darmstadt, Germany). All solutions were prepared in ultrapure water gained from a Millipore Alpha-Q water purification system (Bedford, MA, USA). The whole blood control material (Seronorm 201505) purchased from Promochem (Wesel, Germany), was used for quality assurance. All experimental solutions of varying pH were made using 0.1 M HCl/NaOH.

The His-CQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method [36] with some modifications. Briefly, the mixture of histidine (2 g) and sodium citrate (4 g) in 25 mL Millipore water was heated in a Teflon coated autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h and cooled down to room temperature after the reaction finished. The water was then completely evaporated (at 80 °C), and obtained solid was heated for an additional 2 h at 220 °C. The prepared His-CQDs were cooled down to room temperature and re-dispersed in Millipore water, and stored at 4 °C for further characterization and use. A schematic overview of the preparation of the His-CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

Fluorescence spectra were acquired using a Perkin Elmer LS 55 photo Luminescence spectrometer (MA, USA) with photomultiplier array detector and pulsed Xenon flash lamp at line frequency 50 Hz over 200–1100 nm spectral range with spectral accuracy of 1 nm. The fluorescence measurements were performed in a quartz cuvette of 10 mm optical path length cell at ambient temperature. The fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of His-CQDs was determined using quinine sulfate as a standard fluorophore, and the quantum yield was estimated using the following equation:
(1)
ϕ S
=
ϕ R
×
(
I S
/
I R
)
×
(
1 -
10
-
A R
1 -
10
-
A s
)
×
(
n S 2
/
n R 2
)
where I is the area under the emission peak, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, n is the refractive index of the solvent at 298 K, and the subscripts R and S denote the respective values of the reference and sample [36].

UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer Lambda 25 spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, Beaconsfield, UK) in a quartz cuvette. The morphology of the as-prepared His-CQDs was assessed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, 200 keV, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR) spectroscopic studies were performed on a Bruker VECTOR 22 spectrometer (Bruker Optik GmbH, Ettlingen, Germany). Particle size measurement was examined by employing dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a ZetaSizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, England). The pH of samples was adjusted by a pH Meter 780 (Metrohm, Herisau, Switzerland). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were done on an X-ray BesTec XPS system (Germany) with an AlKα X-ray source (hυ = 1486.6 eV).

First, 0.18 g of the as-prepared His-CQDs was dispersed in 1 mL Millipore water and shaken completely. Afterward, the aqueous solution of His-CQDs was titrated by the successive addition of Mn(II) standard solution, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at λem = 534 nm, where λex = 476 nm. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) is defined as the signal of the optical sensor, where F and F0 denote the fluorescence intensity of as-prepared His-CQDs in the presence and absence of Mn(II), respectively. The sensing measurements were performed in an aqueous solution (pH adjusted to 10 using HCl and NaOH). Each sample was measured at least three times, and the average data were used.

The method developed here was applied to determine Mn(II) in whole blood samples. A 300 µL of each whole blood sample was digested by the addition of 600 μL of 70% nitric acid and heated at 80 °C for 5 min. The digested solution was diluted to a final volume of 1 mL. Then, the total concentrations of Mn(II) ions were determined at the same conditions.

The geometry optimization of histidine and its complexes with the Cr(II), Cr(III), Cu(II), Fe(II), Cd(II), and Mn(II) was carried out employing the density functional theory (DFT) method at the B3LYP level and the combined basis set of LanL2DZ for Cr, Cu, Fe, Cd, Mn and 6–31+G(d,p) for other atoms. All computational calculations were accomplished using the Gaussian 09W (Revision D.01) software package [37]. Moreover, GaussView 5.0.9 [38] was used to visualize the optimized structures.

The synthesis of His-CQDs probe was carried out in an aqueous solution via the hydrothermal method, as described in the Experimental section. TEM, DLS, FT–IR, XPS as well as UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques were carried out to prove the successful synthesis of the His-CQDs. Morphology of His-CQDs was spherical according to TEM results shown in Fig. 2 A. The particles are highly dispersed and uniform in size. As observed in Fig. 2A, the average size of the His-CQDs was obtained at about 4.3 nm as measured using TEM image. Besides, DLS confirmed the formation of nanoparticles with an average size of 6.2 nm (Fig. 2B). The difference between the average size measured using DLS and TEM originates from the fact that DLS approach measures the hydrodynamic radius of the particle [39].

The emissive property of the CQDs and, as a consequence, the operation of the sensor can be tuned by altering the surface and core modification. The core structure, as well as the functionality of the as-prepared His-CQDs, was verified by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2C). FT-IR spectrum of His-CQDs represents three characteristic absorption bands at 1090, 1268, and 1403 cm−1 arising due to the COO− stretching of the carboxylate anion, C-N stretching vibrations of histidine, and NH3 + symmetric deformation. In addition, other adsorption bands at 1593 cm−1, 2861 cm−1, and 2928 cm−1, which can be assigned to C=C, -C-H, and =C-H, were also observed. A broad peak around 3400 cm−1 could correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH functionalities [40,41].

To understand the elemental composition of the His-CQDs and further confirm the results obtained from FT-IR spectroscopy, XPS technique was used. The XPS survey spectrum of the His-CQDs (Fig. 2D) exhibited C1s (284 eV), N1s (400 eV), and O1s (531 eV) peaks, which originated from histidine and citrate ion. Also, Fig. S1 shows the deconvoluted XPS spectra of the C1S, N1s, and O1s regions. In Fig. S1A, the high-resolution C1s spectrum can be divided into three peaks, and the peak positions are located at 284.2 eV, 285.7 eV and 287.9 eV, which can be assigned to C–C, C=C and C–O/C=O species, respectively. On the N1s XPS spectrum in Fig. S1B, the C1s spectra contain two components at 397.8 and 399.3 eV, attributed to C–N and C=N species, respectively. As it was established from FT-IR analysis, the observed nitrogen signal in XPS analysis originated from surface functionality, not from doping. From Fig. S1C, the peaks located at 530.8 eV and 532.1 eV on the high-resolution Ols spectrum can be respectively assigned to C–O and C=O species. Similar observations have been reported [36].

To analyze the optical properties of His-CQDs, UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were employed. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of His-CQDs (Fig. 2E) shows a high energy absorption peak at 271 nm, corresponding to the π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and two shoulders at 368 nm and 553 nm due to n → π* transition of the C=O and C=N bonds, which further confirms the conjugation of histidine to the surface of CQDs [42]. The fluorescence emission spectrum of His-CQDs shows a single peak at 534 nm upon excitation at 476 nm (Fig. 2F). Also, we have studied the UV–Vis absorption spectra of pristine histidine and sodium citrate (See Fig. S2). The absorption spectrum of histidine shows a peak at 218 nm, corresponding to the π → π* transition. Howbeit, the absorption spectrum of sodium citrate shows no peaks in the UV–Vis region. The fluorescence quantum yield of the His-CQDs was determined to be 61.8%. A similar CQDs was synthesized by Pandit et al., who obtained a fluorescence quantum yield of 62% [36].

Furthermore, Fig. 2G indicates the fluorescence emission spectra of His-CQDs with different excitation wavelengths from 360 to 600 nm. To better illustrate the optical properties of the probe, the variation of fluorescence intensity (Curve 1) and emission wavelength of His-CQDs (Curve 2) with different excitation wavelengths were plotted, as shown in Fig. 2H. Notably, fluorescence intensity was increased with increasing excitation wavelengths from 360 to 460 nm (Curve 1). Additionally, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the probe varies from 494.5 nm to 594.0 nm, with a dependence on the excitation wavelengths from 360 to 600 nm (Curve 2). Also, the obtained results from excitation-dependent emission spectra of His-CQDs confirm that new fluorescence emission peak was not observed with the different excitation wavelengths. Similar behavior has been previously reported for the optical properties of amino acid functionalized carbon dots [36].

Furthermore, it is of great significance for human health and bioscience to use hydrophilic fluorescence probes for the detection of Mn(II) concentration in biological media. In this context, the hydrophilic property of the His-CQDs fluorescence probe was studied by dissolving His-CQDs into the mixtures of water and different solvents, including ethyl acetate, chloroform, and n-hexane. As can be seen from the bright green emission of His-CQDs under UV irradiation, the prepared fluorescence probe has a good dispersion ability in water (Fig. S3).

Consequently, the TEM, DLS, FT–IR, XPS, as well as UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy results strongly confirm the successful synthesis of the His-CQDs. It should be emphasized that the as-prepared CQDs probe was functionalized with histidine. In this context, Weiss et al. have reported the thermal decomposition of the eight standard amino acids (including histidine) [43]. Theyfoundthathistidine is very stable and no decomposition takes place in the range of 20–260 °C. Also, Pandit et al. recently reported in situ synthesis of amino acid functionalized carbon dots via a one-step hydrothermal method [36]. Briefly, citric acid and some the amino acids (including histidine) were heated at 180 °C for 12 h. According to the obtained results from NMR, ζ potential, UV–vis spectroscopy, FT-IR, XRD, and XPS, they confirmed that the amino acids are efficiently conjugated to form functionalized C-Dots.

The stability of the as-prepared His-CQDs was investigated by recording the fluorescence spectra of the probe for 30 days. From Fig. 3 A, after 15 days, the probe has retained 97% of its initial emission, while this percentage was 90% for 30 days. The result indicated that the optical properties of the as-prepared His-CQDs are stable during this time period.

The fluorescence intensity of His-CQDs was carefully examined at various concentrations of NaCl (from 10−3 to 10+3 mmol L−1) to clarify the effect of the ionic strength on the photophysical properties of the fluorescence probe (See Fig. 3B). The results indicate that the optical performance of His-CQDs is stable in the presence of different ionic strength conditions.

To investigate the sensing ability of His-CQDs, the fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared fluorescence probe was recorded in the presence of a wide variety of metal ions (23 µg L−1) as interfering species, including Fe(III), Al(III), Zn(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), Cr(III), Cd(II), Ca(II), Mg (II), As(V), Hg(II), Au(III), Bi(III), Cr(VI), Se(IV), Sn(IV), V(V), Co(II), Ni(II) and Mn(II). The results indicate that the His-CQDs probe can be successfully employed for the selective quantification of Mn(II) (Fig. 4 ). It should be noticed that the presence of Co(II) ion resulted in an obstacle in the Mn(II) determination, as shown in Fig. 4A.

To further evaluate the selectivity of the His-CQDs towards Mn(II) (23 µg L−1), the effect of different metal ions including Ni(II), Co(II), Mg(II), Cu(II), Hg(II), Cr(III), Pb(II), As(V), Na(I), Se(IV), Ca(II), Cd(II), Zn(II) and Fe(III) (2.5 mg L−1) on the fluorescent response of the fluorescence probe was examined under the same experimental procedure as Mn(II) detection. As displayed in Fig. S4, other metal ions exhibited negligible interference to the sensing of Mn(II) by His-CQDs.

The sample pH can affect the fluorescence properties of the probe by altering the surface chemistry of the His-CQDs. Therefore, prior to study the analytical performance of His-CQDs, it was compulsory to evaluate the effect of solution pH on the determination of Mn(II). The variation of fluorescence intensity of His-CQDs in the presence of Mn(II) with solution pH was carefully investigated (Fig. 5 ). The results clearly indicated that the fluorescence intensity increased from pH 6.0 to a maximum at pH 10.0, then decreased. According to the pKa values of histidine (pKa1 = 1.82, pKa2 = 6.00 and pKa3 = 9.17), it is negatively charged at pH 10.0 (See Fig. S5). The electrostatic interaction between the positively charged Mn(II) and the negatively charged histidine is responsible for the observed behavior. His-CQDs were well dispersed and stable in most solutions for pH values from 3 to 12 and exhibited a progressively increasing fluorescence. In the highly acidic regions (pH < 3), QDs are well dispersed in water and display strong the His-CQDs aggregated. However, no change in absorption/emission maxima of the fluorescence of the CQDs was observed. The aggregation of the His-CQDs might be responsible for fluorescence quenching at acidic conditions, which is in agreement with previous reports [44–46]. At the higher pH, NH groups were deprotonated, which restored the defect traps where radiationless recombination could occur, resulting in strong fluorescence under basic conditions [44]. Thus, pH 10 was selected as the optimal pH and used for the following experiments.

Fig. 6 A presents the fluorescence spectra of the His-CQDs probe in the presence of known concentrations of Mn(II). It can be clearly seen that upon the addition of Mn(II), the fluorescence intensity of the His-CQDs probe centered at 534 nm was gradually increased due to the interaction between the probe and Mn(II) ion. As depicted in Fig. 6B, linear dynamic range (LDR) with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9980 was found over the concentration range from 3.50 µg L−1 to 35.5 µg L−1. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 1.85 µg L−1, at a signal to noise ratio of 3σ, where σ is the relative standard deviation of a blank solution (n = 10), which is within the range of Mn(II) levels in whole blood (7–12 µg L−1) [47]. These results powerfully indicated the applicability of the His-CQDs as a promising fluorescence probe in the determination of trace Mn(II) ions.

Table 1 compares the figure of merits of the proposed method with the reported fluorescent sensors for the determination of Mn(II) ions [18,21,48–50]. Although the previously reported methods declared in these works could effectively achieve the determination of Mn(II), there is a scope of improvements in the present work. The LOD of the presented sensor is lower than that of most other fluorescent sensors for Mn(II) ions (Table 1). Notably, the obtained LOD is relatively lower than the normal whole blood Mn level, which reveals the potentiality of His-CQDs for assaying of Mn(II) ions. As seen, the design of this sensor is simple and low-cost. The remarkable biocompatibility and low toxicity are critical for biological and biomedical appliances of carbon quantum dots, particularly in biosensing. Above all, unlike the previous three methods [21,48,50], the current sensor works according to the signal-on mechanism. The QDs sensors based on fluorescence enhancement are more advantageous because factors which could compete with the detection system of a quantum dot quenching sensor are less effective [22].

Note, most metal ions could be converted to their hydroxide forms in high pHs. Hence, at pH 10, we have calculated the minimum concentration of metal ion (MCMI) to produce hydroxide forms. MCMI value is simply calculated from the solubility product constant (Ksp). Table S1 lists the calculated MCMI values to produce hydroxide forms. It should be pointed out that the calculated MCMI values are very low as compared to the corresponding values used in the analytical studies. For example, the minimum concentration of Mn2+ to produce Mn(OH)2 was obtained to be 2.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 (20 µmol L−1), while LDR was found over the concentration range from 0.06 µmol L−1 to 0.65 µmol L−1 (See Table S1, and Table 1).

We predict a proposed CHEF mechanism involving the complexation of Mn(II) ion with the histidine functional group of the His-CQDs probe. This interaction was confirmed by the shift in the N–H vibration frequency to lower wavenumbers in the FT-IR spectrum of the His-CQDs in the presence of Mn(II) (Fig. 7 A), which can be ascribed to the metal–N interaction. In other words, at the pH > 10, the -NH groups of the histidine become more prone to donate the lone pair to a metal ion and form stable coordination complexes. Combining the spectral changes of the His-CQDs in the presence of Mn(II) ions (Fig. 7A) and the response of the fluorescence probe to pH values (Fig. 5) suggest that the histidine functionality can serve as a selective chelating moiety for Mn(II) ions.

According to Fig. 7B, the His-CQDs exhibit the fluorescence intensity at 534 nm. As expected, the fluorescence obviously enhances in the presence of the analyte. When 5 µL of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, 87.6 μg L−1) was added to the solution of His-CQDs with Mn(II), the initial fluorescence intensity of His-CQDs was restored. The obtained result was strong evidence that His-CQDs were regained from complex His-CQDs-Mn(II) in the presence of EDTA.

Fig. S6 indicates the TEM image of the His-CQDs probe after the addition of Mn(II). The His-CQDs in the presence of the analyte has the same morphology as that of the pristine probe, indicating that the morphology of the studied CQDs is preserved.

Furthermore, to gain more information about the mechanism of the interaction between the His-CQDs and metal ions, a computational study was employed. The computational study is an able approach to understand the mechanism of fluorescence response [51,52]. The structures of histidine and the complexes of histidine with the Cr(II), Cr(III), Cu(II), Fe(II), Cd(II), and Mn(II) were fully optimized, as shown in Fig. 7C. In the Cr(II)-Histidine, Cr(III)-Histidine, Cu(II)-Histidine, Fe(II)-Histidine, and Cd(II)-Histidine complexes, it is clearly observed that a square- planar geometry was obtained. In contrast, the Mn(II)-Histidine complex with a tetrahedral geometry was formed. We believe that the different sensing behavior of His-CQDs toward metal ions is the result of the difference in chemical structure between Mn(II)-Histidine and other studied complexes monomers. The computational results were in agreement with the experimental results and proposed mechanism.
It should be emphasized that the extensive mechanism of interaction between His-CQDs and metal ions was not introduced in this work.

The applicability of the proposed method was examined using the whole blood control material (Seronorm 210205). The value found after the entire procedure was 31.9 ± 1.8 μg L−1 (n = 5). The obtained result was in good agreement with the certified values of the control material (32.7 ± 1.5 μg L−1). The accuracy was found to be above 97%. Thus, the described procedure is reliable for the determination of Mn(II) in the whole blood sample.

In summary, we presented a simple carbon quantum dot-based fluorescent probe with high photo and pH stability, aqueous dispersibility, and salt stability. The prepared His-CQDs exhibited a high selectivity and sensing ability for Mn(II) ions. The linear range and LOD of the method were found to be 3.50 µg L−1– 35.5 µg L−1 and 1.85 µg L−1 (at 3σ), respectively. Notably, the His-CQDs showed high selectivity to detect Mn(II) ions in the presence of other metal ions. This fluorescence sensing platform exhibits an outstanding sensing ability to Mn(II) over a wide pH range. An efficient way for the regeneration of His-CQDs was achieved using EDTA. His-CQDs were also successfully applied to determine the accurate amounts of Mn(II) in whole blood control material. Further, the assay developed here is green, highly selective, and quite simple. Meanwhile, the proposed fluorescence sensor is compatible with biological environments. It offers efficient discriminative detection of Mn(II) ions over abundant cellular metal ions, especially Ca(II), so this manganese probe can be used for the visualization of Mn(II) in living cells or tissues.

Elham Mohagheghpour: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Leila Farzin: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Arash Ghoorchian: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Sodeh Sadjadi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Project administration, Writing – original draft. Majid Abdouss: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121409.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Manganese toxicity upon overexposure: a decade in review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.L. O’Neal, W. Zheng, Manganese toxicity upon overexposure: a decade in review, Curr. Environ. Health Rep. 2(3) (2015) 315-328.
